critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Late-winter woodland browser feeding heavily as snow begins melting.
critters.fact: White-tailed deer flash the white underside of their tails to signal danger to others nearby.
critters.activeFact: White-tailed deer move more during early spring thaw, especially at dawn and dusk as food becomes easier to reach.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Large forest mammal beginning to stir as winter loosens its grip.
critters.fact: Black bears have an excellent sense of smell, even better than bloodhounds!
critters.activeFact: Early spring bears often start roaming in search of easy meals after months of winter denning.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Active late-winter predator often heard during breeding season.
critters.fact: Coyotes communicate using howls, yips, and barks to mark territory and coordinate with their pack.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, coyotes often patrol open ground and field edges where melting snow exposes scent trails and prey.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Solitary hunter active around thawing fields and woodland edges.
critters.fact: Red foxes use their bushy tails for balance, communication, and warmth during cold weather.
critters.activeFact: As March snow melts, red foxes often work field edges where voles and mice become easier to hear and catch.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Secretive woodland fox moving more during the seasonal transition.
critters.fact: Gray foxes are excellent climbers, using trees to escape predators and search for food.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, gray foxes may shift between den cover and feeding areas as nights stay cold but days warm up.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Elusive predator still hunting hard through the late-winter thaw.
critters.fact: Bobcats use their tufted ears to enhance hearing, helping them hunt small animals quietly at night.
critters.activeFact: As trails soften and snow recedes, bobcats often travel wooded edges where prey movement increases.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow-moving tree feeder still working winter browse in early spring.
critters.fact: Porcupines' quills detach easily to deter predators, but the quills don't shoot out like arrows.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March porcupines are often still focused on woody browse before greener foods fully return.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Wetland engineer becoming more visible as ice loosens on ponds and creeks.
critters.fact: Beavers build lodges with underwater entrances to stay safe from predators and harsh weather.
critters.activeFact: Early spring thaw can trigger fresh beaver activity along shorelines as open water expands.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Aquatic rodent showing more shoreline activity during thaw season.
critters.fact: Muskrats build dome-shaped lodges from vegetation and mud in wetlands for shelter and protection.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, muskrats often cruise open pockets of water and shoreline runs looking for fresh plant material.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Semi-aquatic hunter working thawing banks and open water edges.
critters.fact: Minks are excellent swimmers, using webbed feet to hunt fish and amphibians in water.
critters.activeFact: As March ice breaks apart, mink patrol creek edges and pond margins where prey gets more exposed.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Forest carnivore still roaming widely through late-winter woods.
critters.fact: Fishers are skilled climbers, often hunting squirrels and birds in trees during the night.
critters.activeFact: During the spring transition, fishers often use wooded corridors and ridges as snow conditions change day to day.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Aquatic predator enjoying more open water as winter fades.
critters.fact: River otters can close their ears and nostrils underwater to keep water out while swimming.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March otters often use melting channels and soft banks to travel, hunt, and play near open water.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Tree squirrel busy with feeding and nest activity as spring nears.
critters.fact: Eastern Gray Squirrels use over 200 vocal sounds to communicate with each other in the wild.
critters.activeFact: On mild March days, gray squirrels spend extra time gathering food and chasing through treetops.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Territorial woodland squirrel defending food caches into early spring.
critters.fact: American Red Squirrels fiercely defend their territory and store thousands of seeds in hidden caches for winter.
critters.activeFact: As snow melts back, red squirrels often work old middens and stash spots with their usual tiny attitude.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Ground squirrel reappearing more often on warmer late-winter days.
critters.fact: Chipmunks have cheek pouches to carry food back to their burrows for storage.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March chipmunks may dart out on sunny days, especially near logs, brush piles, and old burrow entrances.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Forest hare caught between winter cover and the coming spring change.
critters.fact: Snowshoe hares change fur color seasonally, turning white in winter for camouflage in snow.
critters.activeFact: The late-winter thaw can leave snowshoe hares standing out more as camouflage lags behind changing conditions.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Common rabbit feeding along brushy edges as snow cover shrinks.
critters.fact: Eastern Cottontails use unique white tails to signal danger and communicate with other rabbits.
critters.activeFact: As snow melts, cottontails often shift to feeding lanes that open up around yards, hedges, and field edges.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Nocturnal forager becoming more active as nights slowly moderate.
critters.fact: Raccoons have highly sensitive front paws, allowing them to open locks and solve complex puzzles easily.
critters.activeFact: During mid-March thaw, raccoons start ranging more widely at night around water, woods, and easy feeding spots.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Cold-sensitive marsupial more visible on milder spring-leaning nights.
critters.fact: Opossums are immune to many snake venoms, including rattlesnake and cottonmouth bites.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, opossums often take advantage of thawing ground and leftover food sources around edges and yards.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Night forager especially active during late-winter breeding season.
critters.fact: Striped skunks can spray a foul-smelling liquid up to 10 feet to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March is prime time for extra skunk movement, especially on mild nights when thawing ground boosts activity.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: Gliding nocturnal squirrel still active in chilly early-spring woods.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels glide using a special membrane called a patagium that stretches between their limbs.
critters.activeFact: Even during the late-winter thaw, flying squirrels stay mostly hidden by day and busy overhead after dark.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Insect-eating bat waiting on warmer nights and emerging insect life.
critters.fact: Little Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects mid-air with incredible precision.
critters.activeFact: By mid-March, warm spells can trigger limited bat activity near roosts if enough insects are moving.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Hardy bat species capable of early-season activity on mild nights.
critters.fact: Big Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects, often eating hundreds of mosquitoes in one hour!
critters.activeFact: A warm mid-March evening can bring big brown bats out to hunt early-season insects near buildings and lights.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Woodland owl vocal during the late-winter to spring transition.
critters.fact: Barred Owls hoot with a distinctive “Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you all?” call.
critters.activeFact: March is a great time to hear barred owls calling through hardwoods after sunset and before dawn.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Powerful owl often already nesting by the time spring begins.
critters.fact: Great Horned Owls have ear tufts that help with camouflage and communication, not hearing.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, great horned owls may already be deep into nesting season while snow still patches the woods.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Bold songbird growing louder and more territorial as spring approaches.
critters.fact: Blue Jays can mimic hawk calls to scare away other birds from their territory.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March blue jays often call loudly, chase rivals, and start acting more serious about territory.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Bright red songbird ramping up territorial singing in early spring.
critters.fact: Male Northern Cardinals sing to defend territory and attract mates with their bright red feathers.
critters.activeFact: One of the clearest signs of spring is a cardinal belting out songs on a cold March morning.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Energetic feeder regular entering spring pair-bonding season.
critters.fact: Black-capped Chickadees can remember hundreds of food cache locations for later retrieval.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, chickadees often switch from quick winter feeding to more calling, chasing, and pairing behavior.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Curious woodland songbird growing more vocal as nesting season nears.
critters.fact: Tufted Titmice have a distinctive crest and often sing a clear, whistled "peter-peter-peter" call.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March titmice often call from exposed perches, sounding off as spring behavior ramps up.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Tree-climbing bird active on warming bark and early nest territory.
critters.fact: White-breasted Nuthatches often climb tree trunks headfirst while searching for insects.
critters.activeFact: As days lengthen, nuthatches often become more vocal and territorial while still working bark for food.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Small woodpecker starting to drum more with spring on the horizon.
critters.fact: Downy Woodpeckers use their stiff tail feathers to balance while pecking on trees.
critters.activeFact: That quick tapping in mid-March often means a downy woodpecker is making itself known.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Large woodland woodpecker more vocal as breeding season nears.
critters.fact: The Hairy Woodpecker uses its long tongue to extract insects from tree bark.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March hairy woodpeckers often work larger trunks and announce their presence with louder, sharper drumming.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Big forest woodpecker becoming more obvious during spring territory season.
critters.fact: Pileated Woodpeckers use powerful beaks to create large rectangular holes in trees for nesting and feeding.
critters.activeFact: A booming call or deep hammering in the March woods can be a pileated woodpecker making headlines.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Common woodland woodpecker growing more active as spring approaches.
critters.fact: Red-bellied Woodpeckers drum on trees to communicate and establish territory.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March red-bellied woodpeckers may spend more time chasing rivals and less time quietly feeding.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Gentle dove beginning courtship behavior as the season shifts.
critters.fact: Mourning Doves can fly up to 55 miles per hour during migration.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, mourning doves may already be pairing up and selecting early nesting spots.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Seasonal finch starting to brighten and shift toward spring behavior.
critters.fact: American Goldfinches molt twice yearly, changing bright yellow in summer and dull olive in winter.
critters.activeFact: By mid-March, goldfinches can look restless and lively as plumage and social behavior begin changing.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-season songbird still common as winter slowly gives ground.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed Juncos change tail feather patterns between regions, aiding in quick identification during birdwatching.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March is often when juncos still dominate the ground under feeders before later spring departures.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Small social finch growing more vocal with the coming nesting season.
critters.fact: House Finches were originally native to the western United States but now thrive across much of North America.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, house finches may shift from simple feeder visits to active singing and pair behavior.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy woodland finch tied to seasonal seed and cone availability.
critters.fact: Male Purple Finches have raspberry-colored heads and chests, making them easy to identify in the wild.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March purple finch activity can come in waves, with short bursts of feeding and song.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Large ground bird starting to shift from winter flocking to spring display season.
critters.fact: Wild turkeys can run up to 20 miles per hour and fly short distances at 55 miles per hour.
critters.activeFact: By mid-March, toms may start strutting more often even if mornings still feel like winter.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Forest bird approaching the spring drumming season.
critters.fact: Ruffed Grouse create a distinctive drumming sound by rapidly beating their wings to attract mates.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March is when the woods can start carrying that distant engine-like thump of a drumming grouse.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall wetland bird returning north with the early migration wave.
critters.fact: Sandhill Cranes perform elaborate, synchronized dances to strengthen pair bonds and communicate with each other.
critters.activeFact: A sandhill crane call over thawing marshes is one of the loudest signs that spring is waking up.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Smart black bird active across open country and woodland edges in early spring.
critters.fact: American Crows can recognize human faces and remember those who pose a threat for years.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, crows often gather loudly at dawn and patrol fields, roads, and timber edges for food.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Large intelligent corvid active in late-winter skies and forest country.
critters.fact: Common Ravens can mimic sounds from their environment, including human speech and other animals.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March ravens may be seen soaring, calling, and working together over openings and wooded ridges.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Soaring raptor entering nest season as winter loosens.
critters.fact: Red-tailed Hawks use their keen eyesight to spot prey from over a mile away while soaring high.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March hawks may spend more time soaring, calling, and carrying sticks than simply hunting.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Fast woodland hawk moving into spring territory behavior.
critters.fact: Cooper’s Hawks are expert fliers, maneuvering quickly through trees to catch birds and small mammals.
critters.activeFact: During mid-March, Cooper’s hawks may make quick, aggressive flights while defending early nest space.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Common duck returning to open water and pairing up for spring.
critters.fact: Mallard ducks can sleep with one eye open to stay alert for predators.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March mallards often form pairs and work newly opened water for food and resting spots.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Colorful cavity-nesting duck returning with early spring wetlands activity.
critters.fact: Wood Ducks nest in tree cavities and are one of the few ducks with brightly colored, iridescent feathers.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, wood ducks may appear on flooded timber, creek bends, and quiet ponds as ice gives way.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Migratory goose active around thawing water and early nest sites.
critters.fact: Canada Geese can fly up to 40 miles per hour during migration.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March geese can get extra noisy and territorial around open ponds, fields, and marsh edges.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Large wading bird returning to open water as thaw expands.
critters.fact: Great Blue Herons can hunt by standing still for minutes, then striking quickly to catch fish.
critters.activeFact: A heron standing beside a half-thawed stream is one of spring’s quieter comeback scenes.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Basking turtle waiting for the first dependable warm-up of spring.
critters.fact: Painted Turtles can live both in water and on land, often basking in the sun to regulate body temperature.
critters.activeFact: On a warm mid-March afternoon, painted turtles may appear on logs even while nights still freeze.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Large aquatic turtle still mostly quiet but beginning to stir as waters warm.
critters.fact: Snapping turtles have powerful jaws that can snap shut with incredible speed and strength.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, snapping turtles may begin shifting from deep winter inactivity toward slow spring movement.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Panfish beginning to adjust to warming shallow water.
critters.fact: Bluegills can change color to communicate mood or during spawning season.
critters.activeFact: During the spring thaw, bluegill often hold near cover where warming water and food start to build.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Freshwater predator easing toward pre-spawn spring movement.
critters.fact: Largemouth bass can change color to blend with their surroundings for better camouflage while hunting.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March bass are often not fully shallow yet, but they start positioning closer to warming cover.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Schooling fish entering one of the earliest spring spawning periods.
critters.fact: Yellow Perch have distinctive vertical stripes and are popular for sport fishing in North America.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March perch may gather in schools near shallower water in preparation for spawning.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Fast predator moving into one of its prime spring spawning periods.
critters.fact: Northern Pike can strike prey with remarkable speed, often lunging from hidden underwater vegetation.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March is right in the wheelhouse for pike movement toward flooded shallows and soft spawning habitat.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny frog preparing for the first true chorus nights of spring.
critters.fact: Spring Peepers are tiny frogs known for their loud, high-pitched mating calls heard during early spring evenings.
critters.activeFact: A mild mid-March evening can trigger the first peeper calls, even if winter hasn’t fully packed up yet.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Large pond frog still mostly quiet while waiting for warmer spring water.
critters.fact: American Bullfrogs can produce loud, deep croaks that can be heard over 1 mile away.
critters.activeFact: In mid-March, bullfrogs are often still in slow mode while peepers and other early callers steal the show.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Common shoreline frog beginning to stir as wetlands warm.
critters.fact: Green frogs can produce a distinctive, banjo-like call heard during warm summer evenings.
critters.activeFact: By mid-March, green frogs may begin shifting from winter stillness toward more shoreline movement.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Spotted wetland frog among the earlier spring movers.
critters.fact: Leopard frogs can jump over 3 feet in a single leap to escape predators quickly.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March leopard frogs may appear on mild days near thawed marshes before many later amphibians arrive.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: Harmless snake often among the first reptiles seen in spring.
critters.fact: Garter snakes can swim and are often found near water hunting amphibians and fish.
critters.activeFact: A sunny mid-March day can bring garter snakes out to bask near brush piles, rocks, and old logs.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Semi-aquatic snake waiting on warmer wetland conditions.
critters.fact: Water snakes can stay underwater for up to an hour while hunting or avoiding predators.
critters.activeFact: By mid-March, water snakes may show limited movement on especially sunny days near open water.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Woodland snake beginning to reappear as spring slowly builds.
critters.fact: Eastern Milk Snakes mimic venomous rattlesnakes with their coloration to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March milk snake sightings are often tied to warm afternoons around old wood, barns, or brushy debris.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Migratory butterfly still mostly south of Michigan as spring begins.
critters.fact: Monarch butterflies use magnetic fields and the sun to navigate thousands of miles during migration.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March is more about the northbound journey beginning than monarchs already filling northern fields.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Large butterfly waiting for steadier spring warmth in the North.
critters.fact: Swallowtail butterflies use their tail-like wing extensions to confuse predators and avoid attacks.
critters.activeFact: By mid-March, swallowtails are mostly still a promise of spring rather than a common sight in northern woods.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Aerial predator still mostly developing underwater this early in the season.
critters.fact: Dragonflies can fly up to 30 miles per hour and hover like helicopters while hunting.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March wetlands hold future dragonflies below the surface long before summer patrol flights begin.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Slender wetland insect still mostly in aquatic juvenile stages during March.
critters.fact: Damselflies fold their wings over their bodies when resting, unlike dragonflies that keep wings open.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, damselflies are more hidden in pond margins than hovering over them.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Summer glow insect still dormant during the March thaw.
critters.fact: Fireflies use bioluminescence to attract mates and communicate through species-specific light patterns.
critters.activeFact: March is still way too early for blinking firefly nights, but the groundwork is quietly there underfoot.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Pollinator taking advantage of the first mild spring flights.
critters.fact: Honeybees communicate by dancing to share the location of food sources with their hive mates.
critters.activeFact: A sunny mid-March afternoon can bring honeybees buzzing out briefly even while frost still owns the mornings.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Cold-tolerant bee among the earliest pollinators to reappear.
critters.fact: Bumblebees can regulate their body temperature by vibrating their flight muscles, allowing them to fly in cold weather.
critters.activeFact: Mid-March warmth can bring out lone queen bumblebees searching for flowers and a nesting spot.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Jumping insect still mostly absent from the March scene in northern woods.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers use their powerful hind legs to jump up to 20 times their body length.
critters.activeFact: This part of the year is still quiet for grasshoppers, with spring thaw setting the stage long before summer hopping begins.
